key_points: Light travels in straight lines and allows us to see objects when it reflects into our eyes
Reflection occurs when light rays strike a surface and bounce back
A plane mirror forms a virtual, upright image that is the same size as the object
Concave mirrors can converge light rays and form magnified or real images depending on object distance
Convex mirrors diverge light rays and always form smaller virtual images
Lenses refract light and can converge or diverge light rays
examples: Plane mirrors used in dressing mirrors
Concave mirrors used in shaving mirrors to produce enlarged images
Convex mirrors used as rear-view mirrors in vehicles
Convex lenses used in magnifying glasses
Concave lenses used in spectacles for correcting short-sightedness
formulas: Law of reflection → Angle of incidence = Angle of reflection
Reflection rule → Incident ray, reflected ray, and normal lie in the same plane
Refraction → Bending of light when it passes from one medium to another
exam_tips: Understand the differences between concave and convex mirrors
Remember common uses of mirrors and lenses in daily life
Practice simple ray diagrams for mirrors and lenses
Explain reflection and refraction with examples in answers
